message.content:
Adversaries may exploit Login Hooks to gain persistence by executing scripts with root privileges upon user logon. This involves modifying the com.apple.loginwindow.plist file to include a path to a malicious script using the LoginHook or LogoutHook key-value pair. These hooks require administrator permissions to create or modify and are executed at the next user login or logout. Although deprecated in macOS 10.11 in favor of Launch Daemons and Launch Agents, Login Hooks can still be found in older systems. The potential impact includes unauthorized access and execution of malicious code with elevated privileges, leading to system compromise.

Common exploitation methods include using the defaults command-line utility to alter the plist file and inserting additional commands into existing hooks. Mitigation strategies involve monitoring and restricting access to plist files, ensuring only authorized users have administrative privileges, and regularly auditing system configurations for unauthorized changes. Additionally, upgrading to the latest macOS versions can help mitigate the risk associated with deprecated features like Login Hooks.
